does: This paper develops a theoretical framework for certifying when sparse autoencoder (SAE) reconstructions faithfully explain language model behavior. The authors treat SAE-based interpretation as a post-hoc generalization problem where a frozen LM is replaced with a sparse proxy obtained by substituting native activations with SAE reconstructions. They derive a PAC-style upper bound on the original model's expected risk decomposed into four measurable terms: proxy risk on downstream tasks, SAE reconstruction error, concept-pool mismatch between the sparse feature space and task-relevant concepts, and sparse model complexity. The bound becomes non-vacuous (tighter than vacuous bounds) on GPT-2 Small, Gemma-2B, and Llama-3-8B, demonstrating practical applicability at realistic sample sizes with layerwise empirical analysis.
connects: This work bridges interpretability and learning theory by extending classical generalization bounds to the setting of neural network proxies and feature extraction. It builds on recent work using SAEs for mechanistic interpretability of LMs, responding to fundamental questions about faithfulness that have motivated post-hoc interpretation frameworks in the broader explainability literature. The approach combines ideas from complexity-based learning theory (PAC bounds, Rademacher complexity) with modern interpretability concerns about whether extracted sparse features actually capture the model's computational mechanisms. It also relates to prior work on evaluating feature importance through proxy models and understanding reconstruction-fidelity tradeoffs in bottleneck architectures.
enables: This certification framework enables principled evaluation of whether SAE-based explanations merit trust for downstream applications like model auditing, safety analysis, and mechanistic understanding. Non-vacuous bounds could guide decisions about when sparse reconstructions are sufficiently faithful for making claims about LM internals. The approach opens avenues for improving SAE training by optimizing against the bound's components, developing better mismatch metrics for concept discovery, and extending certification to other feature extraction methods beyond SAEs. It also provides a foundation for certifying multi-layer explanations and studying how faithfulness propagates through deep architectures, potentially enabling more rigorous mechanistic interpretability research.
